Chapter 11 of McKinney, W., Python for data analysis; data wrangling with pandas, NumPy and iPython, O’Reilly, available at VLeBooks. | [Python for data analysis at VLeBooks](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1449444) | 

# Book Time Series

Descriptive statistical methods are

- Measures of central tendency(mean, median, mode)
- Measures of dispersion(range, sd, variance)
- Measures of distribution(skewness, kurtosis)

Inferential statistics uses samples to infer properties of population

- Hypothesis testing
- Regression analysis
- Probability distributions with confidence intervals

The statistical approach to time series uses techniques such as

- Autoregression
- Moving average
- Exponential smoothing

Statistical models are better for transparency and efficiency.

ML are useful for complex data and very large datasets.

Both use regression of one form or another.

Statistical methods

- ARIMA
- Combination(SES, Holt, and Damped)
- Damped exponential smoothing(DES)
- Exponential smoothing(ETS)
- Holt
- Seasonal Random Walk(NAive2)
- Simple Exponential Smoothing(SES)
- Theta

Machine Learning methods

- Bayesian Neural Network(BNN)
- Classification and Regression Tree(CART)
- Gaussian Process(GP)
- Gated Recurrent Neural Network(GRNN)
- K Nearest Neighbour(KNN)
- Long Short Term Memory(LSTM)
- Multi Layer Perceptron(MLP)
- Radial Basis Function(RBF)
- Recurrent Neural Network(RNN)
- Support Vector Regression(SVR)

Symmetric mean absolute percentage error(SMAPE) was used as the main performance metric.

![SMAPE = 100 /n ( >= |Ft - At| /( ([At] + |Ft|)/2 
where At is the actual value and Ft is the forecast value.](data:image/png;base64,)

[https://blog.reachsumit.com/posts/2022/12/stats-vs-ml-for-ts/](https://blog.reachsumit.com/posts/2022/12/stats-vs-ml-for-ts/)

Best models were statistical models. Only two ML were better than any statistical models, these were BNN and MLP.

With latest models, ML barely beats the statistical models but require way too much compute.

Multivariate uses multiple variables, can be more robust, but can suffer from overfitting.

One approach for multivariate time series forecasting is Vector Autorepression(VAR). It requires lots of data and careful selection of lag length.

Other statistical methods are Vector-ARMA(VARMA) and Gaussian Processes(GP).

It can be difficult for statistical models and ML models to capture complex non-linear relationships in larg-scale time series.

A typical method in ML is Support Vector Regression(SVR) Recurrent Neural Networks(RNN) and Long Short Term Memory(LSTM)

## Support Vector Regression

Extension of SVM, that attempts to find a linear function f(x)=w⊤x+γ that it can approximate with a tolerance ϵ using a training set (X, y) where y∈R. 

In case the function does not exist, slack variables ξi+≥0 and ξi−≥0 denoting positive and negative deviations from the desired tolerance are introduced and minimised, similar to the soft-margin SVM.

So it works for regression continuous values rather than classification like SVM

## Recurrent Neural Network

Captures the weights from the immediately preceding cases to feed into predictions for the next case.

![The image illustrates the architecture of a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN). On the left, there is a single cell (A) with a loop, indicating that the output at each time step (h sub t) is fed back into the cell. On the right, this is unrolled into a sequence, showing multiple cells (A) connected in a chain. Each cell takes an input (x sub t) and the hidden state from the previous time step, producing the output hidden state (h sub t). The unrolled sequence demonstrates how the RNN processes a sequence of inputs over time, maintaining a hidden state that captures information from previous steps.](data:image/png;base64,)

## Long Short Term Memory

Forget date, update gate and output gate

Memory cell updated with a tanh activation function.

Final output goes to a softmax function.

![LTSM architecture as described in the preceding text.](data:image/png;base64,)

### Preparing the data

- Data cleansing: Remove errors from the data
- Temporal aspects: Maintain the order of the data points in time, and account for seasonality and trends
- Missing value: Handle missing values
- Stationarity: Ensure the data is stationary
- Lag features: Engineer lag features into the data. So like yesterdays sales or last weeks sales are features
- Exploratory data analysis: Use visual representations to highlight the main characteristics of the data.
- Data reduction: Limit the number of features or variables in the data
- Data validation and consistency checks: Ensure the data is of high quality and consistent.

### Framing the data as a supervised learning problem

How far back you look int time is known as the lag.

So X will be the previous value and y is the current value - so supervised learning.

![X 
y 
45 
40 
Training data 
40 
39 
Training data 
39 
40 
Training data 
40 
46 
Training data 
46 
51 
Training data 
51 
48 
Training data 
48 
11 
Training data 
44 
40 
Training data 
40 
38 
Training data 
38 
? 
Test data](data:image/png;base64,)

The ML might be good if the data is stationary but not if there are trends.

Should use stationarity and differencing to prepare time series forecasting with ML, as to remove the trend.

To capture seasonality, the lag should be the number of time units in a season. Which you can see by graphing the data.

Exogenous variable and be included as additional independent variables.

Differencing is often best practice, but there are alternatives.

You could include time as an independent variables, which can capture trends just moving in one direction. But an undulating time series pattern can render it ineffective.(wavy pattern)

You can use a sliding window pattern, the size of the lag/season.

![The sliding window approach, as described in the preceding text. The sliding window moves along until the end of the series when a final prediction is made.](data:image/png;base64,)

How far it moves along each time is the stride.

![X1 
X2 
X3 
y

48

38 
? 
Table 1: Time series dataset - Lag 3, Stride 1](data:image/png;base64,)

Popular time series packages

[https://opentimesseries.com/](https://opentimesseries.com/)

Statsmodels library includes statsmodels.tsa. Includes univariate autoregressive models(AR), vector autoregressive models(VAR), univariate autoregressive moving average models(ARMA), Non-linear models include Markov switching dynamic regression and autoregression.

Other popular libraries

- Pmdarima
- Auto_TS.Auto_TimeSeries, let's you use ARIMA, SARIMAX, VAR, decomposable(trend, seasonality,  and holidays) models, and ensemble ML models
- Prophet is an open-source library by meta for forecasting time series data based on additive regression model, It has a high-level interface and is user-friendly, providing automatic forecasts.

### Machine Learning methods

- Sktime is a library that provides a unified interface, time series classification, regression, clustering, annotation, and forecasting.Scitkit-learn compatible tools.
- Darts, forecasting an anomaly detection on time series
- Pandas

- Resample - to convert and resample a time series.
- Diff - returns a dataframe with the difference between the values for each row and by default the previous row, can be used for differencing
- Shift - shifts values of a dataframe along a specified axis, useful for creating lags
- Tshift - shifts the time index of a dataframe, using the index's frequency if available.
- Rolling - performs rolling window calculations on sequential data
- Ewm - provides exponentially weighted calculations. It can be used for exponential smoothing.

### Dates and times

[https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

- Datetime module - useful for manipulating dates and times
- Time module - time access and conversions
- Zoneinfo - create time zones representing IANA time zone database
- DateType - Third party library the intorduces static type, differentiate between aware and naïve datetimes, as in do they include timezone info.

No trend

There is seasonality

Chapter 11 of McKinney, W., Python for data analysis; data wrangling with pandas, NumPy and iPython, O’Reilly, available at VLeBooks. | [Python for data analysis at VLeBooks](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1449444) | 

The text exposes date and time manipulation. It also uncovers some of the Pandas methods mentioned in this topic.
